Baptist Health Louisville
4000 Kresge Way
Louisville KY 40207-4605
Noble, Anella
MRN: [PHONE-1287], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F
Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021
[DOB-108] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
L&D Delivery Note (continued)
Findings: Amniotic Fluid clear
Uterus normal
Tubes and ovaries normal bilaterally
Infant:
Gender: Viable male infant
Weight: 3695 g (8 lb 2.3 oz)
Apgars: 4 @ 1 minute /
8 @ 5 minutes
Indication for C/Section: Failure to Progress
Procedure Details: The patient was taken to the operating room where epidural
anesthesia was found to be adequate. She was prepped and draped in the usual sterile
manner in the dorsal supine position with leftward tilt. A Pfannenstiel incision was made
and carried down to the fascia. The fascia was incised in the mid-line and extended
transversely with Mayo scissors. The fascia was grasped and elevated and separated
from the underlying rectus muscles superiorly and inferiorly. The peritoneum was
identified and entered. Peritoneal incision was extended superiorly and inferiorly with
good visualization of the bladder. The bladder blade was inserted and the vesicouterine
peritoneum was incised transversely and the bladder flap was created digitally. The
bladder blade was reinserted. The lower uterine segment was incised in a transverse
fashion. The head was elevated and delivered through the incision. The remainder of
the infant was delivered without difficulty. The umbilical cord was clamped and cut and
the infant was handed off the field. Cord ph and cord bloods were obtained. The
placenta was removed intact and appeared normal. The uterine incision was closed with
running locked sutures of 0 monocryl. The abdominal cavity was irrigated and cleared of
all clot and debris. The uterine incision was inspected and noted to be hemostatic.
Rectus muscles were reapproximated in the midline with 0 chromic. The fascia was
closed with 0 PDS in running continuous fashion. The subcutaneous tissue was closed
with 3-0 chromic. The skin was closed in subcuticular fashion with 4-0 Monocryl.
Instrument, sponge, and needle counts were correct prior the abdominal closure and at
the conclusion of the case. Mother and baby to recovery room in stable condition.
Complications: none

PT called for BF assistance.She currently has baby latched to left beast. Mom reports some pinching. Educated her on
starting nose to nipple to obtain deep latch,mportance of deep latching and how to achieve it, also how to know if baby
is getting enough. Adjusted baby's latch with chin tug and mom reports that latch feels better.Good jaw rotation
noted.Encouraged to call LC if needing further assistance
This note was copied from a baby's chart.
Mother has some blistering on both nipples, reports some pinching with latch. Infant currently latched to left nipple,
upon release nipple lipstick shaped. Discussed the importance of asymmetrical latch and how to achieve deep
latch. Assisted mother with football positioning on right side, demonstrated deep asymmetrical latch. Mother reports
no pain or pinching with latch. Discussed how to determine nutritive sucks and audible swallows noted. Encouraged
mother to provide support and guidance to nipple for infant to achieve proper latch. Discussed when to expect milk
to come in and ways to tell infant is getting enough. Encouraged mother to call for assistance or questions as
needed.
This note was copied from a baby's chart.
Mother reports that she has been able to feed infant a few times this afternoon and the latch is feeling better. She
denies any additional nipple damage. Encouraged mother to call for assist as needed.
This note was copied from a baby's chart.
P1 term baby. Mom getting ready for D/C. She reports her breast are feeling fuller now and baby is nursing better.
She denies any questions. Discussed how to know baby is getting enough milk and discussed OPLC, given card.
Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 46

Baptist Health Louisville
4000 Kresge Way

Noble, Anella
MRN: [PHONE-119], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F
Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021
[DOB-120] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
Clinical Notes (group 1 of 2) (continued)
Dr Lewis to bedside after RN request for MD to review strip. MD discussed with patient the need for IUPC
placement and amnioinfusion to resolve variable decelerations. MD also voiced concern about size of baby and
slow cervical change. MD mentioned possibility of cesarean section if labor does not progress. Patient states she
really does not want to have a c section. MD agreeable to allowing labor to continue and reassess cervix in a
couple of hours.
Late entry:
OR case started at 1848. FOB was told that we will observe moment of silence as much as possible upon delivery
of baby. FOB verbalized understanding that if there were any complications with baby, there would not be absolute
silence. Birth time was at 1855. Baby was still on abdomen and FOB tried to walk around to the sterile field after
being asked by AA (Dr Lammers) to stay seated. I asked the FOB to sit down as well and he pushed his body
toward me to get me to move. I put my hands in front of my body to keep him away from me.
 Baby went directly to warmer for resuscitative measures (see notes on delivery record) where three nurses
including NNP were assisting in care. FOB was getting loud and trying to push his way toward baby in order to
whisper his prayer in baby's ear. I told him that baby needed assistance breathing at the moment and that he can
sit right where he was (less than 1 foot away from baby) and say his prayer. FOB tried to push his way to warmer
which is when I put my hands in front of my body again and told him he will have to leave the OR if he does not sit
down. Dr Lammers stood up and at the same time Dr Pennell came into OR. FOB sat down.
FOB stayed seated after this but continued to interrupt resuscitative efforts by making rude comments toward staff
and doctors. Breathing efforts toward baby continued until 10 minutes. FOB said more than one prayer during this
time with no attempt at interruptions of prayer by any staff.
FOB signed visitor behavior contract with security officer; Stating that going forward any aggressive, threatening
behavior or interference with pt or infant care he will be removed from the hospital and not allowed back during the
pts stay. House Manager, Risk and MB Unit Manager made aware.
Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 47
Electronically signed by Hicks, Kara, RN at 01/30/21 0410
Summary: Father's behavior in OR
Electronically signed by Houlihan, Jennifer L, RN at 02/03/21 1040
Nursing Note

Baptist Health Louisville
4000 Kresge Way
Louisville KY 40207-4605
Noble, Anella
MRN: [PHONE-121], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F
Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021
[DOB-1272] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
Clinical Notes (group 1 of 2) (continued)
Late entry
Summary of day
Patient called out requesting information about epidural at 1100. This RN explained the epidural process. Patient
states she would like an epidural at this time. Patient's significant other states " Do not give it to her unless I tell
you. She doesn't really want it." This RN explained to the patient's significant other that it is the patient's choice and
that I will get anesthesia if the patient asks for an epidural. He proceeded to tell me "leave the room because it's not
your decision." The patient states that she was okay to discuss with her significant other.
Dr. Sherman was at bedside at 1128 for epidural placement. The patient's significant other was told to take a seat
during the procedure and he responded " I wont pass out, I've killed people before so I'm not sitting down."
At 1634, Dr. Terrassa was at bedside to discuss plan of care with patient and significant other. MD discussed risks
and benefits of cesarean delivery. The patient's significant other stated " We will not be doing a c-section, I believe
in God over science."
At 1645 the patient's significant other came out to the nurses station to talk with this RN in the hallway. The
patient's significant other told this RN that they would refuse the cesarean section if we could not promise a minute
of silence at delivery. Discussed with significant other normal protocols for our staff in a c-section and why it is
important for us to be able to vocalize amniotic fluid color, baby birth time, bleeding, etc but that we would try to limit
these to accommodate for a moment of silence for prayer. This RN told the significant other that we could not
promise that because each delivery is different and we can not predict how the baby will come out at delivery and if
he would need special attention due to patient risk factors. The significant other then proceeded to say that "we will
risk the baby and mom's life if I can not say a prayer to my son and the room cannot be silent. My son's first words
that he hears will not be coming from you all." This RN told him we would do everything we can to make that
possible but can not promise.
At 1655 The patient's significant other stated to this RN " If we do the c-section I do not think the patient needed any
other numbing medicine." This RN explained that the c-section is abdominal surgery and we would have to give her
stronger medicine so she was comfortable during the procedure. He proceeded to say " My family has addiction
problems and you will not make my baby addicted to any medications." This RN told him that is part of the c-section
and that I can get anesthesia to the room to talk about medications if they decide to proceed with the c-section.
At 1659 the patient called out and said she would like to proceed with a cesarean delivery at this time. This RN
again explained that we would try to have a minute of silence at delivery but could not promise at this time. Also
explained no video in the OR was allowed and Significant other would have to be seated during the entire
procedure behind the sterile drape. He would not be allowed to stand or go into the sterile field. Both patient and
significant other agreed to this.
Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 48

Safety Promotion/Fall Prevention: clutter free environment maintained
Goal: Optimal Comfort and Wellbeing
Outcome: Ongoing, Progressing
Intervention: Provide Person-Centered Care
Recent Flowsheet Documentation
Taken 2/1/2021 2310 by Parrott, Kimberli, RN
Trust Relationship/Rapport: care explained
Goal: Readiness for Transition of Care
Outcome: Ongoing, Progressing
Goal Outcome Evaluation:
Plan of Care Reviewed With: patient
Goal Outcome Evaluation:
 Meeting goals. VSS. Pt. Claims good pain relief with medicines. Incision healing well. No s/s of infection. No falls. Pt.
Showing good bonding with infant.
Tolerating labor well, sleeping between contractions. IUPC placed around 3AM due to recurrent variable decels,
now improved s/p amnioinfusion. At 3AM cervix was 3/90/0. UCs not yet adequate so working to titrate pitocin.
EFW last week per u/s was 8lb5oz so did discuss potential for cephalopelvic disproportion. Will continue to titrate
pitocin and once adequate if no further progress this AM discussed need to consider cesarean delivery
Lauren Lewis, MD
Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 57
Progress Notes

[DOB-1252] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
Clinical Notes (group 1 of 2) (continued)
Reviewed in detail noted slow progress of labor increased risk of PP bleeding due to over saturation of uterine
receptor for prolonged labor and increased risk of infection due to ROM >24. Also, noted moderate caput and
suspect Cephalopelvic Disproportion and rec to proceed with CD to avoid bleeding complications in light of clinically
labor not progressing and now seeing decrease variability in FHT's. Pt given time to ask questions and will like to
discuss with FOB who mentioned that "he does not believe in science and he will like to let God decide". Also, that
having a CD will not allow him to do what he will like to do/ Reviewed in detail that I even if she is having minimal
cervical change I suspect that patient will have failure to descend in light of pelvic exam and progression of labor/
BH Louisville

[DOB-155] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
Clinical Notes (group 1 of 2) (continued)
Patient Name: Anella Noble MRN: [PHONE-156]
Today's Date: 2/2/2021
Admit Date: 1/28/2021
Discharge Needs Assessment
No documentation.
Discharge Plan
Row Name 02/02/21 1037
Plan
Plan Infant to discharge home with mother when medically ready. Deena Green,
CSW
Plan Comments Mother: Anella Noble, MRN [PHONE-1237]; Infant: AnellasBoy “Alik” Noble, MRN
[PHONE-158]. CSW was consulted for “Social support,” and “hx of abuse per
report, family dynamics concerns.” Of note, no urine toxicology obtained on
mother nor infant. Cord toxicology was not sent. No indication of need for
toxicology screens at this time. CSW spoke with RN Mary Jude M, who advised
that father is controlling and loud, but not violent or threatening. CSW met with
mother alone at bedside. Mother verified address, phone, and insurance.
Mother reports she has spoken with MedAssist about adding infant to insurance
coverage. Mother reports that she and father are in the process of moving, but
mother did not disclose new address. Mother reports they have car seat, crib,
clothes, diapers and other supplies. Mother is not current with WIC, is breast
feeding. CSW educated mother about WIC program. Mother plans on follow up
with Dr. Kahttah and is comfortable scheduling appointments and will have
transportation to appointments. Mother reports that maternal grandmother is
involved and supportive. Mother denies any violence, threats, or feeling unsafe.
HANDS program discussed with mother, mother agreeable to HANDS referral.
CSW made HANDS referral via secure email. Mother was cooperative and
polite during discussion. Mother with bright affect and good eye contact during
discussion. Mother denied any other needs or concerns. CSW provided mother
with packet of resources info and briefly discussed resources in packet. Packet
includes info on: HANDS, WIC, domestic violence resources, infant supply
resources, transportation, counseling, and general community resources.
Deena Green, CSW
Continued Care and Services - Admitted Since 1/28/2021
Coordination has not been started for this encounter.

Acknowledged: Sylvia, Kayla, RN 01/29/21 0054 for Placing Order Culver, Amy Christine, RN 01/30/21 2309 for D/C Order
Admin instructions: Give IV push if:
Maternal systolic blood pressure drops below 100 mmHg or 10% drop from
baseline
Fetal heart rate deceleration (20 beats per minute below baseline lasting
more than 30 seconds) occurs with in 20 minutes of epidural dosing
Discuss dosing with Anesthesia in patients with pre-eclampsia,
hypertension, cardiac history or tocolytic
Caution: Look alike/sound alike drug alert Dilute with NS to 5-10 mg/mL.
 Central line preferred, if unavailable use large bore IV access with
frequent nurse monitoring of IV site.

Admin instructions: Start at 2 milli-units/min and titrate up or down by 2 milli-units every
30 min to a max of 20 milli-units/min. Titrate to maternal/fetal
tolerance, with a goal of achieving an adequate labor pattern defined by
progressive cervical effacement and cervical dilation of approximately 0.5
cm/hr to 1 cm/hr once active labor is achieved. Contractions should not be
more frequent than every 2 minutes. Notify physician for tachysystole.

Order comments: NO TREATMENT RECOMMENDED IF: 1) Maternal GBS Status Known Negative 2) Scheduled Cesarean Birth
With Intact Membranes, Not in Labor 3) Maternal GBS Status Unknown, No Risk Factors TREAT WITH ANTIBIOTICS IF: 1)
Maternal GBS Status Known Positive 2) Maternal GBS Status Unknown With Risk Factors: a) Previous Infant Affected By GBS
Infection b) GBS Urinary Tract Infection (UTI) or Bacteriuria During Pregnancy c) Unexplained Maternal Fever (100.4F (38C) or
Greater) During Labor d) Prolonged Rupture of Membranes (18 or More Hours) e) Gestational Age Less Than 37 Weeks

Order comments: Indwelling Urinary Catheter Removal Criteria Discontinue Indwelling Urinary Catheter Unless One of the Following
is Present: Urinary Retention or Obstruction Chronic Urinary Catheter Use End of Life Critical Illness with Strict I/O GU Tract or
Abdominal Surgery Stage 3/4 Sacral / Perineal Wound Required Activity Restriction: Trauma Required Activity Restriction: Spine
Surgery If Patient is Being Followed by Urology Contact Them PRIOR to Removal Do Not Remove Indwelling Urinary Catheter Order
is Present with a CLINICAL REASON to Maintain the Catheter. Provider is Required to Include a Clinical Reason to Maintain a
Urinary Catheter Patient Admitted With Indwelling Urinary Catheter (Not Placed at Baptist Facility) Assess for Continued Need &
Document Medical Necessity If Infection is Suspected, Contact the Provider

Frequency: Routine 02/03/21 - Class: Clinic Performed
Quantity: 1
Order comments: Nothing vaginally until after seen in office. Bath, shower, stairs ok. Driving is ok when the baby is a week old and if
you are not taking percocet/narcotics. No intentional exercise or heavy lifting over 10 lbs. If something hurts, don't do it. Call for
heavy vaginal bleeding more than soaking a pad/hour, fever of 101. Follow up with Women First in 6 weeks. The office should
contact you to make appointment within a week of discharge.

Outcomes
Intervention: Support Maternal Role Transition (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Develop trust, relationship and rapport.
Use a family-focused approach; promote involvement of support system in infant care.
Incorporate cultural beliefs, rituals and practices in family-centered care.
Encourage and support breastfeeding, frequent holding and skin-to-skin contact with newborn.
Encourage attachment behaviors; promote involvement in infant care.
Monitor maternal emotional state, as well as maternal-infant interaction.
Encourage and answer questions; share resources for support following discharge.
Outcomes
Date/Time User Outcome
02/02/21 1053 Meyer, Mary J, RN Met

Ongoing, Progressing
Intervention: Identify and Manage Fall Risk (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Perform standard risk assessment with a validated tool or comprehensive approach
appropriate to the patient on admission; reassess fall risk frequently, with change in status or
transfer to another level of care.
Communicate fall injury risk to interprofessional healthcare team.
Determine need for increased observation, equipment and environmental modification, such as
low bed and signage, as well as supportive, nonskid footwear.
Adjust safety measures to individual developmental age, stage and identified risk factors.
Reinforce the importance of safety and physical activity with patient and family.
Perform regular intentional rounding to assess need for position change, pain assessment,
personal needs, including assistance with toileting.
Date/Time
Intervention: Prevent Skin Injury (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Assess skin risk on admission and at regular intervals throughout hospital stay.

Goal: Optimal Comfort and Wellbeing (Resolved)
Intervention: Prevent and Manage VTE (venous thromboembolism) Risk (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Assess for VTE risk.
Encourage/assist with early ambulation.
Initiate and maintain compression or other therapy, as indicated based on identified risk in
accordance with organizational protocol/provider order.
Encourage both active and passive leg exercises while in bed, if unable to ambulate.
Outcomes
Date/Time User Outcome
02/02/21 1053 Meyer, Mary J, RN Met
02/01/21 2357 Parrott, Kimberli, RN Ongoing, Progressing

Intervention: Monitor Pain and Promote Comfort (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Assess pain level, treatment efficacy and patient response at regular intervals using a
consistent pain scale.
Consider the presence and impact of preexisting chronic pain.
Encourage patient and caregiver involvement in pain assessment, interventions and safety
measures.
Intervention: Prevent Infection (Completed)
Intervention: Provide Person-Centered Care (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Use a family-focused approach to care.
Develop trust and rapport by proactively providing information, encouraging questions,
addressing concerns and offering reassurance.
Acknowledge emotional response to hospitalization.
Recognize and utilize personal coping strategies.
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Maintain skin and mucous membrane integrity; promote hand, oral and pulmonary hygiene.
Optimize fluid balance, nutrition, sleep and glycemic control to maximize infection resistance.
Identify potential sources of infection early to prevent or mitigate progression of infection (e.g.,
wound, lines, devices).
Evaluate ongoing need for invasive devices; remove promptly when no longer indicated.
Keep all areas of skin (especially folds) clean and dry.
Maintain adequate skin hydration.
Relieve and redistribute pressure and protect bony prominences; implement measures based
on patient-specific risk factors.
Match turning and repositioning schedule to clinical condition.
Encourage weight shift frequently; assist with reposition if unable to complete independently.
Float heels off bed. Avoid pressure on the Achilles tendon.
Keep skin free from extended contact with medical devices.
Use aids (e.g., slide boards, mechanical lift) during transfer.

Intervention: Mutually Develop Transition Plan (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Identify available resources for support (e.g., family, friends, community).
Identify and address barriers to ongoing treatment and home management (e.g.,
environmental, financial).
Provide opportunities to practice self-management skills.
Assess and monitor emotional readiness for transition.
Establish/reconnect linkage with outpatient providers or community-based services.
Honor spiritual and cultural preferences.

Intervention: Monitor and Manage Intrapartum Bleeding (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Facilitate position change, if possible, to enhance maternal circulation and increase uterine
blood flow (lateral position with hip wedge).
Maintain large-bore intravenous access; provide fluid resuscitation as indicated by maternal
status.
Continue fetal heart rate monitoring until delivery.
Note presence of bleeding when membranes are ruptured; test for presence of fetal blood.
Assess bleeding amount; check underpads for presence of blood pooling; weigh pads for
measurement accuracy (1gm equals 1mL).
Verify readiness and accessibility of blood products if transfusion needed.
Administer oxygen therapy in the presence of maternal hypoxia.
Prepare for expedited delivery with change in maternal or fetal status.
Prepare for uterotonic medication administration for bleeding management following delivery.

Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary
Goal: Hemostasis (Resolved)
Description: Facilitate position change, if possible, to enhance maternal circulation and increase uterine
blood flow (lateral position with hip wedge).
Maintain large-bore intravenous access; provide fluid resuscitation as indicated by maternal
status.
Continue fetal heart rate monitoring until delivery.
Note presence of bleeding when membranes are ruptured; test for presence of fetal blood.
Assess bleeding amount; check underpads for presence of blood pooling; weigh pads for
measurement accuracy (1gm equals 1mL).
Verify readiness and accessibility of blood products if transfusion needed.
Administer oxygen therapy in the presence of maternal hypoxia.
Prepare for expedited delivery with change in maternal or fetal status.
Prepare for uterotonic medication administration for bleeding management following delivery.
Anticipate need for surgical intervention with uncontrolled bleeding (e.g., uterine compression
sutures, vessel ligation or embolization, hysterectomy).
Maintain calm, reassuring approach with patient and family; answer questions; provide
information and timely responses to assist with anxiety reduction.

Assess bleeding amount; check underpads for presence of blood pooling; weigh pads for
measurement accuracy (1gm equals 1mL).
Verify bladder has been emptied; encourage voiding, perform catheterization if unable to void.
Anticipate need for vaginal exam to determine presence of lacerations or hematoma.
Watch for changes in blood pressure, heart rate and urine output.
Anticipate administration of uterotonic medication.
Prepare for additional bleeding-control measures (e.g., balloon tamponade, vaginal packing).
Anticipate rapid infusion of intravenous fluid and blood product administration.
Verify readiness and accessibility of blood products if transfusion needed.
Monitor for coagulopathy and signs of abnormal bleeding (e.g., oozing from intravenous sites,
petechiae).

sutures, vessel ligation or embolization, hysterectomy).

Intervention: Promote Breast Care and Comfort (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Optimize nipple and skin integrity; avoid use of alcohol or soap solutions; utilize topical agents
such as colostrum, breast milk.
Mutually establish acceptable comfort level; evaluate pain using consistent tool or self-report
description.
Encourage use of nonpharmacologic comfort measures, such as warm or cold applications,
breast massage, manual expression of milk and use of well-fitting support bra; offer analgesia
if needed.
Monitor and manage signs of breast or nipple infection; advocate for treatment of mother and
infant if nipple is infected.
Encourage continued breastfeeding or breast pumping, even when pain, engorgement,
blocked duct or an infection is present; provide reassurance and guidance.
Encourage adequate maternal hydration, nutrition and rest.
Intervention: Promote Effective Breastfeeding (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Mutually develop a feeding plan; include father/partner/support person.
Promote cue-based and infant-led feeding; goal of 8 to 12 times per 24-hour period.
Provide ongoing support during feeding sessions to enhance maternal infant experience.
Adjust environment to provide calm surroundings and privacy; limit distractions.
Assist with recognition of and response to infant feeding readiness and satiety cues;
demonstrate infant rooting and sucking reflexes.
Demonstrate techniques to maintain infant’s feeding readiness state, such as unwrapping and
gentle tactile or vocal stimulation.
Assist with effective positioning, latch and breast attachment techniques (e.g., move infant to
breast versus breast to infant, stimulate suck with drop of colostrum or breast milk).
Observe feedings at minimum twice daily, assessing infant’s ability to latch, suck and swallow,
mother infant positioning and presence of milk transfer; evaluate infant for signs of adequate
intake (e.g., number of wet diapers and stools).

Intervention: Promote and Monitor Fetal Wellbeing (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Evaluate fetal heart rate tracing.
Facilitate maternal lateral position change to improve uteroplacental blood flow and maternal
blood pressure, utilize hip wedge as needed.
Monitor uterine contraction pattern; assess for presence of tachysystole.
Anticipate need for tocolytic administration if tachysystole persists.
Prepare for intravenous fluid bolus administration to improve maternal fluid status and treat
hypotension.
Administer oxygen therapy for maternal hypoxia.
Prepare for expedited delivery if fetal status does not improve.
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Discuss and reinforce benefits for infant and mother.
Initiate uninterrupted breastfeeding within 1 hour of birth; promote skin-to-skin contact.
Advocate for patience and persistence; encourage breastfeeding in the presence of difficult
circumstances; eliminate or minimize separation of infant-mother and encourage rooming-in.
Initiate pumping of breast milk within 6 hours of birth if unable to nurse.
Assist with breast pumping, proper handling and storage of breast milk.
Advocate for the use of alternative breast milk feeding methods when breastfeeding is
medically not possible (e.g., cup, finger, feeding tube device).
Discourage use of a pacifier until breastfeeding is well-established.
Assess support system integrity; emphasize the importance of father/partner/support person
involvement and use of available resources.
Discuss future feeding plans and assist with barrier resolution (e.g., return to work, birth
control).

Frequency: Per CPG

Description: Evaluate fetal heart rate tracing.
Facilitate maternal lateral position change to improve uteroplacental blood flow and maternal

Monitor uterine contraction pattern; assess for presence of tachysystole.

fetal stimulation).
Modify pushing effort, if in second stage of labor.
Prepare for expedited delivery if fetal status does not improve.

Intervention: Prevent or Manage Infection (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Protect and secure insertion site; observe for signs of infection, sepsis or line dislodgement.
Provide meticulous dressing and catheter care to decrease risk of infection; maintain closed
system.
Optimize fluid balance, nutrition, sleep and glycemic control to maximize infection resistance.
Identify potential sources of infection early to prevent or mitigate progression of infection (e.g.,
wound, lines, devices).
Obtain cultures prior to initiating antimicrobial therapy. Do not delay treatment for laboratory
results in the presence of high suspicion.
Administer ordered antimicrobial therapy promptly; reassess need regularly.
Provide fever-reduction and comfort measures.
Note: Maternal fever can occur during neuraxial analgesia/anesthesia that is not associated
with infection. Source of temperature elevation should be investigated.
01/30/21 2049 Hicks, Kara, RN Ongoing, Progressing
01/30/21 2012 Robb, Amber, RN Ongoing, Progressing
02/01/21 2357 Parrott, Kimberli, RN Ongoing, Progressing
Intervention: Maintain Anesthesia/Analgesia Safety (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Optimize patient position for insertion accuracy (e.g., sitting, lateral decubitus); consider hip
wedge to displace uterus and avoid vessel compression.
Maintain fetal heart rate monitoring.
Implement safety measures (e.g., verify solution/flow rate/pump setting when infusion starts,
dose changes or shift changes, use portless infusion tubing, use pumps that prevent free-flow,
recheck/trace tubing connections, label tubing).
Prepare for intravenous fluid bolus administration prior to initiation of anesthesia to avoid
hypotension.
Prepare for induction of neuraxial anesthesia/analgesia agent; advocate for test dose and
anticipate reversal agent.
Maintain supportive environment; promote relaxation (e.g., offer reassurance, provide
information, answer questions).
Anticipate urgent medical management for hypotension, block at high level or medication
toxicity.
Verify intact catheter tip with removal.

Intervention: Prevent or Manage Intraamniotic Infection (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 01/30/21
Description: Verify Group B streptococcus status and presence of known infection.
Assist with obtaining cultures, as indicated.
Limit digital vaginal exams or invasive vaginal procedures, especially after membranes are
ruptured.
Prepare to initiate antimicrobial therapy for suspected or confirmed intraamniotic infection.
Promote perineal hygiene.
Provide fever-reduction and comfort measures, as needed.
Notify neonatal team for delivery.
Anticipate need for single-dose antimicrobial therapy prophylaxis following anal sphincter
injury.
Prepare to send placenta for histology following delivery, as indicated.
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Verify Group B streptococcus status and presence of known infection.
Prepare to administer antimicrobial therapy prophylaxis within 60 minutes prior to delivery,
unless there is current coverage provided by existing antimicrobial therapy regimen.
Note: If cesarean delivery is urgently needed, prophylaxis should be administered as soon as
possible following incision.
Assist with obtaining cultures, as indicated.
Limit digital vaginal exams or invasive vaginal procedures, especially after membranes are
ruptured.
Notify neonatal team for delivery.

Intervention: Prevent or Manage Infection (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Optimize fluid balance, nutrition, sleep, oxygenation, glycemic control and body temperature to
maximize resistance.
Maintain dressing and closed drainage system integrity to reduce the risk for infection.
Consider splinting techniques to prevent incision dehiscence.
Discontinue prophylactic antimicrobial agent within 24 hours after surgery, as directed.
Identify potential sources of infection early; evaluate continued need and advocate for early
removal (e.g., lines, devices, drains).
Implement transmission-based precautions and isolation, as indicated, to prevent spread of
infection.
If infection suspected, administer ordered antimicrobial therapy promptly; reassess need
regularly.
Note: If endometritis is suspected, treatment may be initiated without obtaining cultures.
Identify and manage signs of early sepsis.
Provide fever-reduction and comfort measures.

Intervention: Prevent or Manage Pain (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 01/30/21
Description: Determine pain management plan with patient and caregiver; review plan regularly.
Use a consistent, validated tool for pain assessment; evaluate pain level, effect of treatment
and patient’s response at regular intervals; along with standard pain scales, the patient can
also be asked how she is coping with the pain.
Consider the presence and impact of preexisting chronic pain.
Encourage patient and caregiver involvement in pain assessment, interventions and safety
measures.
Individualize pharmacologic pain management plan; titrate medication to patient response.
Prepare for initiation of analgesia/anesthetic, such as neuraxial or inhaled; patient-controlled
neuraxial analgesia may be used.
Monitor and address medication-induced effects, such as constipation, nausea, vomiting,

Description: Develop mutual pain management plan with patient and caregiver; review regularly.
Monitor for the presence of incisional pain; provide timely pain management interventions
when present.
Use cold application, as culturally-appropriate, to the incisional area for the first 24 to 48 hours
to enhance comfort.
Encourage early ambulation, when able, to help avoid gas accumulation which can add to
discomfort.
Verify correct infant latch when breastfeeding to prevent nipple pain.
Consider the presence and impact of preexisting chronic pain.
Encourage patient and caregiver involvement in pain assessment, interventions and safety
measures.
Individualize pharmacologic pain management plan; titrate medication to patient response.
Combine multimodal analgesia and nonpharmacologic strategies to help potentiate synergistic
effects and enhance comfort (e.g., complementary therapy, diversional activity).
Offer around-the-clock dosing of pain medication to keep pain levels in control.
Manage medication-induced effects, such as constipation, nausea, vomiting.
Support and optimize psychosocial response to pain.
If engorgement occurs, encourage more frequent breastfeeding or pumping and storing
additional milk to ease discomfort.
Note: Cold compresses, as culturally–appropriate, may be used if bottle-feeding.
If post-dural puncture headache identified, encourage adequate hydration and anticipate the
need for epidural blood patch.
If hemorrhoids are present and painful, offer topical pain relief and sitz baths for comfort.

Description: Reassess fall risk frequently and with change in status or transfer to another level of care.
Communicate fall injury risk to interprofessional healthcare team. (e.g., rounds change of
shift/provider, patient transport).
Anticipate needs; perform regular intentional rounding to assess need for position change, pain
assessment, personal needs (e.g., toileting) and placement of necessary items.
Promote use of personal vision and auditory aids (e.g., glasses, hearing aids).
Assess assistance level required for safe and effective self-care; provide support as needed
(e.g., toileting, mobilization).
Define behavior and activity limits to patient and family to decrease fall or drop risk.
If fall occurs, assess for and treat injury; determine cause; revise fall injury prevention plan.
Regularly review medication contribution to fall risk; consider polypharmacy and high-risk
medications (e.g., antiepileptic, narcotic, regional block, anesthesia, sedation) given within last
24 hours.
Adjust medication administration times to minimize risk of falling.
Balance adequate pain management with potential for oversedation

Intervention: Optimize Oxygenation and Ventilation (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Use a validated screening tool to identify risk for obstructive sleep apnea prior to placement;
monitor for signs of hypoventilation.
Implement continuous pulse oximetry to detect apnea-related oxygen desaturation events.
Monitor fluid balance to promote optimal hydration.
Assess for bladder distension; encourage voiding within 6 hours following removal of
indwelling catheter and regularly thereafter.
Promote behavioral methods to enhance voiding ability (e.g., utilize relaxation techniques,
mutually establish a regular elimination schedule, allow time for bladder emptying, position to
optimize flow, encourage double voiding).
Implement methods to facilitate elimination (e.g., running water, warm water over perineum,
sitz bath, privacy).
Utilize portable bladder ultrasound to assess pre- and post-void volumes.
Anticipate the need for intermittent catheterization; advocate for early removal of indwelling
device.
Promote urogenital hygiene to prevent skin breakdown.
Promote early mobilization to improve return of urinary tract function.

Description: Reassess skin injury risk and inspect skin frequently (e.g., scheduled interval, with turning, with
change in condition) to provide optimal prevention.
Maintain adequate tissue perfusion (e.g., encourage fluid balance, avoid crossing legs,
constrictive clothing or devices) to promote tissue oxygenation.
Maintain head of bed at lowest degree of elevation tolerated, considering medical condition
and other restrictions. Limit amount of time head of bed is elevated greater than 30 degrees to
prevent friction/shear injury.
Avoid positioning onto an area that remains reddened.
Minimize incontinence and moisture (e.g., toileting schedule; moisture-wicking pad, diaper or
incontinence collection device, skin moisture barrier).
Cleanse skin promptly and gently when soiled utilizing a pH-balanced cleanser.
Relieve and redistribute pressure (e.g., schedule position changes; utilize higher specification
foam mattress, chair cushion, constant low-pressure or alternating-pressure support surface;
medical device repositioning; protective dressing applicatio
Support increased progressive functional activity (e.g., therapeutic exercise) to decrease risk
associated with immobility. Balance rest with activity.

Intervention: Monitor and Manage Uterine Activity (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 01/30/21
Description: Monitor uterine contraction activity and fetal heart rate during labor process.
Facilitate maternal position change to lateral with hip wedge as necessary.
Prepare to administer intravenous fluid bolus.
Anticipate need to adjust or discontinue pharmacologic therapy (e.g., cervical ripening agent,
oxytocin).
Prepare for oxygen administration in the presence of maternal hypoxia.
Prepare to administer tocolytic agent if uterine activity fails to normalize.
Reassess uterine activity and fetal heart rate for response to interventions.
Prepare for delivery if contraction pattern or fetal heart rate fail to normalize or with change in
maternal-fetal status.
Notify neonatal team of delivery.

Staff and visitors should clean their hands before and after contact with your child/you.
Everyone should clean their hands when coming in and leaving your child's/your room.
Your child/you should wash your hands often and after going to the bathroom.
Special items may need to be worn by anyone coming into your child's/your room, such as a mask, gown and gloves.
Friends and family who are sick should not visit.
Speak up if anyone is not following these rules.
Staff and visitors should use hand cleanser or wash their hands when entering and exiting a room, as well as prior to and
after touching the patient.
Point: Harm Prevention (Resolved)
Safety measures include:
patient identification verified using 2 identifiers
barcode scanning
medication reconciliation
IV lines and tubing tracing
Encourage the patient and family to speak up with any
concerns they have about care.
Your child/you will be asked to tell name and birth date many times.
Your child's/your identification bracelet may be scanned as well. This helps to make sure that the right treatments are given
to the right patient.
If your child/you have any IV lines or tubing, we will be checking them often.
Please tell someone if you have questions or concerns about your child's/your care.
Visitors should wear proper protection when infection-prevention signs are posted.
Speak up if infection prevention principles are not practiced by patients, visitors or healthcare team members.
Friends and family with symptoms of illness should avoid visiting.
Educate patients and family members about the importance of daily hygiene care and special handling of drains, wounds and
dressings.
Description:
Inform the patient and family that there are several ways to make sure the right care is received.
Everyone, including you, can stop the spread of infection.
Patient Friendly Description:

Point: Pain Assessment Process (Resolved)
It is important that pain is managed, so that the patient can be an active participant in the treatment and therapy needed for
his/her recovery.
Inform the patient and family that managing pain is a partnership. It is important to speak up if pain is not well-controlled.
Your child/you will be asked often about pain. Where is it? How bad is it?

Title: Cesarean Delivery Education (Obstetrics) ()
Topic: Text (Resolved)
You are more likely to get an infection the longer you have the tube.
Point: Infection Prevention (Resolved)
The tube will be:
secured to thigh
checked for kinks often
The drainage bag will be:
emptied and measured often
hung below the level of hips, but never on the floor
The care team and should will:
wash hands before and after catheter care
provide perineal care at least daily and after bowel movements
Other ways to prevent infection include:
keep the tube fastened to your upper leg to keep it from being pulled out by mistake
make sure it drains well
keep the drain bag below your hips and off the floor
It will be emptied often to check how much urine is in the bag.
Everyone should wash their hands before and after touching your tube.
Your private area should be washed at least once a day and after you poop.
Point: Description (Resolved)
Risk of infection is directly related to length of time the catheter is in place.
The infection can be in any part of your urinary system.
It could be in your kidneys.
Point: Cesarean Delivery (Resolved)
It could be in the tubes that carry pee (urine) from your kidneys to the bladder.
It could be in your bladder.
It could be in the tube that carries pee (urine) from your bladder to outside of your body.
A catheter associated urinary tract infection (CAUTI) is an infection related to invasive device placement such as a urinary
catheter.
Description:
Patient Friendly Description:
Taking the tube out as soon as you don't need it will help.
The patient should know the catheter will not be left in any longer than needed.
Learning Progress Summary
Patient Friendly Description:
A catheter associated urinary tract infection (CAUTI) is an infection during or after a tube is in your bladder.

Title: Postpartum Cesarean Delivery Education (Obstetrics) ()
Topic: Self-Management (Resolved)
incision care
Ambulating soon after delivery promotes healing and improves well-being.
When getting up from bed, the patient should sit briefly and then stand without walking for a few seconds. This will help to
prevent dizziness or lightheadedness.
Good posture should be maintained when walking to take pressure off her back.
It can take several weeks to feel fully physically recovered.
Encourage rest each day and to gradually increase activity.
Suggest napping when the infant naps.
Visitors and social activities should be limited.
Strenuous work should be avoided.
Lifting should be limited to no more than 10 pounds.
Help should be sought from family/friends for household chores and child care.
Personal hygiene includes:
avoid douching and using tampons until care provider says that it is okay
use a squirt bottle to rinse her bottom front to back after urinating or having a bowel movement; wiping should also be from
front to back
showering frequently once care provider has said that it is okay
Getting up and walking right after the delivery is good for healing.
Sit on the edge of the bed before standing so you won't feel dizzy.
Sleep enough so that you feel rested, balancing activity with periods of rest.
Take naps when your baby sleeps.
Try to keep friends and family from visiting unless they are there to help.
Do not lift anything heavier than 10 pounds.
Avoid exercise until your doctor says that it is safe.
Ask for help with house work or caring for your other children.
Avoid douching or using tampons until your doctor says that is okay.
Use a squirt bottle to rinse your bottom front to back after peeing (urinating) or pooping (having a stool).
Wipe from front to back.
It is okay to shower and bathe frequently.
Point: Self-Care (Resolved)
Self-care following a cesarean delivery includes:
Learning Progress Summary
Patient Friendly Description:
It may take several weeks to feel fully recovered after a cesarean delivery.

Baptist Health Louisville
4000 Kresge Way
Louisville KY 40207-4605
Noble, Anella
MRN: [PHONE-785], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F
Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021
[DOB-786] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
Patient Education (continued)
Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 366
Menstrual-like cramping is expected as uterine involution takes place.
Lochia should progressively decrease in amount; change in color is normal over the next several weeks. Lochia changes from
rubra (1 - 3 days) to pinkish-brown and gradually to alba (white) after postpartum day 10 and for up to 6 weeks. It should
never have a foul odor (may be a signal of infection).
Breast engorgement occurs on postpartum day 2 or 3. If not breastfeeding, breasts should return to pre-pregnant condition
within 1 to 2 weeks after delivery.
Fluid shifting following delivery may cause bladder over-distension. Voiding may also be affected by anesthetic.
Voiding should occur as soon as possible after delivery. If unable to void, straight catheterization may be necessary. With
continued difficulty, a temporary indwelling urinary catheter may be necessary.
Sweating and more frequent urination occurs due to normal fluid shifts during the first week after delivery.
Treatment of Constipation:
It is common for bowel movements to be irregular after delivery.
Stool softeners can help to make the stool easier to pass.
Drink 8-10 8 oz. glasses of water a day.
Eat more high-fiber foods, like fruits, vegetables, whole grain breads, cereals and dry beans.
A mild laxative may be ordered if no bowel movement has passed in a couple of days.
Changes include:
Cramps, like what you feel during a period, as your uterus shrinks a little each day.
Bleeding that will gradually slow and stop.
The bleeding (discharge) will also change in color from red to pinkish-brown to white.
The bleeding or discharge should never smell bad.
You will notice your breasts become swollen and firm with breast milk; this is called engorgement.
This happens 2 or 3 days after birth.
If you are not breastfeeding, a snug bra and ice packs may help you feel better until it gradually goes away.
It is important that you are able to pee (urinate) soon after your baby is born.
If you have trouble peeing you may have to have a catheter (tube in your bladder) put in for a short time.
You may notice that you are going to the bathroom (to pee) more often or sweating more during the first week after birth.
This is normal because your body is getting rid of extra fluid from pregnancy.
It is common to have trouble pooping (having a stool) after your baby is born.
Drink a lot of fluids, especially water.
Eat foods that are high in fiber like fresh fruits and raw vegetables and whole grain breads and cereals.
Your doctor may suggest medicine to help you go if needed.
Point: Expected Physical Changes (Resolved)
You will notice many changes over the next few days as your body returns to a 'before pregnancy' state.

Make sure someone is with you while you feed or care for your baby if you are feeling sleepy or have taken pain medicine.
Point: Expected Psychological Changes (Resolved)
This happens right away for some but may take longer for others. This is normal.
Baby blues” are common during the first few weeks after delivery caused in part by hormone changes, sleep deprivation and
family lifestyle adjustment.
Blues usually resolve within a week after delivery.
Postpartum depression is more serious and longer lasting. It is treatable. It is important to seek help.
Symptoms of a problem should be reported to the care provider immediately.
These include:
mood swings
extreme anxiety
sleep disturbances
repetitive/intrusive thoughts/worry about infant’s welfare
thoughts of harming self or infant
loss of interest in caring for infant
fear of being left alone with infant
Encourage Identification of a personal support system.
Provide information on local postpartum depression support group/behavioral health resource.
Some parents feel a strong attachment to their baby right away. For others it may take longer. Both are 'normal'.
Bonding is a process that happens while you are giving your baby the care that he needs every day. You may not even know
it is happening until your baby smiles for the first time and you feel great love and joy.
It is very common to feel down or cry easily after your baby is born. This is caused by changes in your hormones, lack of
sleep and the hard work of adjusting to a newborn baby. This is called the 'Baby Blues'. You will usually feel better in a week
or so.
If the feelings last longer or get worse, you may be having postpartum depression.
If you have any of these symptoms you should call your doctor right away:
changes in your mood from very happy to very sad
Point: Infant Safety (Resolved)
The infant should not be fed unattended if mother is feeling sleepy or if sedated from pain medicine.
Patient Friendly Description:
When you sleep, put your baby in the crib. You should never sleep with your baby.
Description:
During the postpartum period parents begin bonding or feeling attached to their infant.
Description:
To prevent accidental dropping or injury, the baby should be placed safely in the bassinette when napping and to sleep at
night.
Learning Progress Summary
Patient Friendly Description:
The birth of your baby causes emotions that may be new to you.

It is important that you reach out for help.
Call your doctor right away.
You can also call a family member or friend for support.
Consider counseling and joining a support group for moms with postpartum depression in your community.
feeling irritable or cranky
Point: VTE Prevention (Resolved)
To prevent VTE, early frequent ambulation is encouraged and should be continued at home.
Provider’s instructions for continuing VTE prophylaxis medication (as applicable) should be followed at home.
If you have a blood clot, it will most likely be in one of your arms or legs.
A blood clot can also travel to your lungs. This is known as a pulmonary embolism.
One of the best things you can do to prevent a blood clot is to get up and walk often. You should also do this after you go
home.
If you have been taking special medicine to prevent blood clots, your doctor may want you to keep taking it after you go
home. Take this medicine only as your doctor tell you.
Point: Incision Care (Resolved)
Hands should be washed with soap and water before and after caring for incision.
Generally, the dressing will be removed after 24 hours.
The incision should be kept clean and dry by cleansing with soap and water, rinsing and patting dry to prevent infection.
Taking a shower or soaking in a tub should be avoided until provider says that it is okay.
Underwear or clothing that rubs or places pressure on the area should be avoided.
The incision should be exposed to air as much as possible, particularly if it located under a skin flap.
Wash your hands with soap and water before and after taking care of your incision.
The dressing will likely be removed after 24 hours.
Unless you are told differently, cleanse the incision gently with soap and water, rinse and pat dry.
Do not take a shower or soak in a tub until your doctor tells you it is okay.
sleeping too little or too much
thoughts of hurting yourself or your baby.
Follow what your doctor's tells you for how to take care of your incision.
Description:
Patient Friendly Description:
Description:
Instruct patient to follow provider's instructions for incision/site care.
Being pregnant and delivering a baby can increase your chances of getting a blood clot.
Pregnancy and the postpartum period greatly increase the risk of developing VTE.

Title: Labor Education (Obstetrics) ()
Topic: Overview (Resolved)
Topic: Text (Resolved)
Do not wear underwear or clothes that rub or put pressure on the your incision.
Let air get to the incision as much as possible, especially if it is under a flap of skin.
Point: Cesarean Delivery, Care After (Resolved)
Point: Description (Resolved)
First stage:
begins with regular uterine contractions and ends with complete cervical dilatation at 10 cm
Second stage:
begins with complete cervical dilatation and ends with the delivery of the infant
Third stage:
period between the delivery of the infant and the delivery of the placenta and fetal membranes
During labor, the baby, bag of water, umbilical cord and placenta are come out of the womb (uterus).
There are three parts to labor.
The first begins when labor pains start and ends when you are fully dilated.
The second ends with the baby's birth.
The third part ends after the placenta (afterbirth) is passed.
Labor is the process of childbirth.
Description:
Description:
Labor is the process during which the fetus, membranes, umbilical cord and placenta are expelled from the uterus. There
are three stages.

Topic: Self-Management (Resolved)
pelvic pressure with the urge to push
pain that starts as a backache
labor pains that are spaced evenly apart
some pink or light red mucus discharge from the birth canal (bloody show)

You will notice that the labor pain gets get stronger, lasts longer and gets closer together.
Just before delivery, you may feel pressure and want to push (bear down).
Point: Signs/Symptoms (Resolved)
Point: Labor Care (Resolved)
If there is a birth plan, it should be reviewed with the patient and family.
Contractions will be monitored throughout the labor process.
Electronic fetal monitoring will be used to monitor fetal status.
Control of pain during labor may be accomplished with a variety of methods (e.g., relaxation and breathing techniques,
position changes, analgesia and/or anesthesia).
As labor progresses, position changes can be expected.
If there are positions that will better facilitate delivery and are not contraindicated, they should be utilized.
Support during labor should be encouraged.
regular contractions that increase in intensity, frequency and duration
passing of cervical mucus plug, bloody vaginal discharge (bloody show)
discomfort usually beginning as a dull ache in lower back
rupture of amniotic membranes
Symptoms of labor may include:

Title: Breastfeeding Education (Adult,Obstetrics) ()
Topic: Self-Management (Resolved)
Point: Pacifiers and Formula (Resolved)
Non-nutritive sucking with a pacifier may benefit the preterm infant.
Milk substitutes (formula) can lead to decreased breast milk supply and alter the benefits of breast milk for the infant.
Your baby might not breastfeed as well.
Sometimes, when a baby is born early, the doctor will want the baby to use a pacifier.
Giving your baby formula instead of breast milk can keep him/her from getting the good things that breast milk provides.
Talk to your baby's doctor before giving your baby a pacifier or formula.
Point: Infant-Led Feeding (Resolved)
The infant should be breastfed:
when he/she shows signs of being hungry (feeding on demand)
to reduce the fullness of his/her mother's breasts
until he/she is done (infant-led feeding schedule)
The infant should be allowed to feed on each breast for as long as he/she wants.
In the first few weeks following birth, the infant may be sleepier and need to be awakened for a feeding.
As a guide:
newborns may breastfeed every 1 to 3 hours
newborns should not go longer than 3 hours without a feeding during the day
newborns should not go longer than 5 hours without a feeding during the night
Point: Pain Relief During Labor and Delivery (Resolved)
Patient Friendly Description:
Patient Friendly Description:
Description:
Frequent feedings will help the mother make more milk.
Giving your baby a pacifier when you are breastfeeding can cause him/her to get mixed up. A pacifier nipple feels different
than your nipple.
Education for You
Learning Progress Summary
Learning Progress Summary
Description:
Handout for Patient
Description:
Pacifier use is linked to breastfeeding difficulties (nipple confusion, poor latch) in full-term infants.

Let your baby feed from both breasts for as long as he/she wants (baby-led feeding schedule).
Your baby may be very sleepy right after he/she is born (first 2 weeks). You may need to wake him/her up for feeding.
New babies:
will need to be breastfed every 1 to 3 hours
should not wait longer than 3 hours before being fed during the day
should not wait longer than 5 hours before being fed during the night
Babies should be fed at least 8 times a day (within 24 hours).
infants should be fed at least 8 times in a 24 hour period
Point: Breast Care (Resolved)
To prevent nipples from becoming dry, cracked and sore, the mother should:
wash her breasts every day
avoid using soap on her nipples
air-dry her nipples for 3 to 4 minutes after a feeding
wear a supportive bra
use only cotton bra pads to absorb leaking breast milk
use lanolin or expressed breast milk on her nipples after feeding to keep them from getting too dry
ask her doctor before using breast creams or breast shells
Wash your breasts every day.
Try not to use soap on your nipples.
Air-dry your nipples for 3 to 4 minutes after you feed your baby.
Wear a good bra that supports your breasts.
Use only cotton pads in your bra to soak up leaking breast milk.
Use lanolin or expressed breast milk on your nipples after feeding to keep them from getting too dry.
Ask your doctor before using breast creams or breast shells.
Point: Breast Milk Supply (Resolved)
Breastfeed your baby whenever he/she acts hungry (feeding on demand).
You will make more milk if you feed your baby many times a day.
Learning Progress Summary
Patient Friendly Description:
Patient Friendly Description:
Nipples can become dry, cracked and sore without proper care of your breasts. This can have a negative impact on your
breastfeeding experience.
Learning Progress Summary
Description:
Advise the mother to:
Description:
Breast care is important for a positive breastfeeding experience.

Eat 3 healthy meals each day.
Massage your breasts and express milk by hand before you pump or breastfeed.
If you are not able to breastfeed, pump at least 8 times each day. This means every 2 to 3 hours during the day and at least

Do not limit how long your baby breastfeeds each time.
drink plenty of fluids and eat 3 healthy meals each day
Point: Infant Positioning (Resolved)
Pillows or blanket rolls should be used for support.
The infant should be brought to the breast rather than the mother leaning forward toward the infant.
Assist the mother in determining the most comfortable and effective hold for her and the infant (e.g., clutch or football, cradle
or across the lap, side-lying, ventral or lying down).
Use pillows or a blanket under your arm while you are feeding your baby.
Bring your baby to your breast, instead of leaning your breast forward toward your baby.
You should decide which way to hold your baby during breastfeeding.
Pick a way that makes you and your baby feel the best.
use breast massage and manual expression (before pumping or breastfeeding)
Point: Adequate Intake (Resolved)
Age 3 to 5 days of life:
infant having at least 3 to 5 wet diapers and 3 to 4 stools per 24 hours
Age 5 to 7 days of life:
infant having at least 4 to 6 wet diapers and 3 to 6 stools per 24 hours
Age 2 to 5 days:
at least 3 to 5 wet diapers every day
at least 3 to 4 dirty diapers every day
pump frequently if unable to breastfeed; 8 times a day (every 2 to 3 hours during the day and at least once at night)
avoid limiting the length of time for each breastfeeding session
Patient Friendly Description:
The way you hold your baby while you are breastfeeding will help him/her eat better. It will make your breasts feel better
too.
Learning Progress Summary
Patient Friendly Description:
You can tell if your baby is getting enough to eat by counting his/her wet and dirty diapers.
Description:
Positioning is important for feeding success.
Patient Friendly Description:
Description:
Adequate intake of breast milk is measured by the infant's amount of urine and stools:
Drink enough fluid so that (pee) urine is clear pale yellow.

Point: Latching/Swallowing (Resolved)
She may hear the infant swallowing between sucks (every 3 to 4 sucks).
She will see muscle movement above and in front of the infant's ears while he/she is sucking.
She should not hear clicking or smacking while the infant is sucking.
She should not have nipple pain while the infant is sucking.
When your baby starts to suck, it should feel like a little tugging or pulling on your breast. It should not hurt.
You may hear your baby swallowing between sucks (every 3 to 4 sucks).
You will see movement by your baby's ears while he/she is sucking.
You should not hear clicking or smacking while the baby is sucking.
You should not have nipple pain while your baby is sucking.
Point: Fluid/Food Intake (Resolved)
Some foods may cause the infant to have gas, making him/her more fussy.
Avoid or limit these foods, which include:
chocolate
spicy food
some vegetables, such as broccoli, cauliflower, cabbage, onions or Brussels sprouts
Avoid or limit caffeine and alcohol.
Avoid eating fish with high mercury content, such as tilefish, shark, swordfish and king mackerel.
Follow specific instructions from provider.
Some foods will give your baby gas, causing him/her to be fussier.
Avoid or limit these foods, which may include:
chocolate
spicy food
some vegetables, such as broccoli, cauliflower, cabbage, onions and Brussels sprouts
Avoid or limit caffeine and alcohol.
Avoid eating fish with high mercury content, such as tilefish, shark, swordfish and king mackerel.
Follow specific diet instructions from your doctor.
at least 4 to 6 wet diapers every day
at least 3 to 6 dirty diapers every day
Patient Friendly Description:
When you are breastfeeding, you are burning 400 - 500 more calories per day than normal. These calories should be
replaced by eating a healthy diet and drinking enough fluids so that your pee (urine) is clear, pale yellow. This will also help
your milk supply.
Description:
The breastfeeding mother will burn about 400 to 500 additional calories each day. The calories should be replaced by
maintaining a healthy diet and drinking enough fluids so that urine output is clear, pale yellow. This will also help to maintain
the mother's milk supply.
Description:
The mother will know the baby has latched effectively by feeling a painless and silent tugging or pulling sensation when the
infant takes her breast.

Patient Friendly Description:
There are several ways you can tell if your baby is attached to your breast well (latch).

Patient Instructions
None
Other Instructions
Discharge Instructions
Nothing vaginally until after seen in office. Bath, shower, stairs ok. Driving is ok when the baby is a week old and if you are not taking
percocet/narcotics. No intentional exercise or heavy lifting over 10 lbs. If something hurts, don't do it. Call for heavy vaginal bleeding more
than soaking a pad/hour, fever of 101. Follow up with Women First in 6 weeks. The office should contact you to make appointment within a
week of discharge.

More Information
Breastfeeding
Choosing to breastfeed is one of the best decisions you can make for yourself and your
baby. A change in hormones during pregnancy causes your breasts to make breast milk
in your milk-producing glands. Hormones prevent breast milk from being released
before your baby is born. They also prompt milk flow after birth. Once breastfeeding
has begun, thoughts of your baby, as well as his or her sucking or crying, can stimulate
the release of milk from your milk-producing glands. Benefits of breastfeeding
Research shows that breastfeeding offers many health benefits for infants and mothers. It also offers a cost-free and
convenient way to feed your baby. For your baby · Your first milk (colostrum) helps your baby's digestive system to function better. · Special cells in your milk (antibodies) help your baby to fight off infections. · Breastfed babies are less likely to develop asthma, allergies, obesity, or type 2 diabetes. They are also at lower
risk for sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS). · Nutrients in breast milk are better able to meet your baby’s needs compared to infant formula. · Breast milk improves your baby's brain development. For you · Breastfeeding helps to create a very special bond between you and your baby. · Breastfeeding is convenient. Breast milk costs nothing and is always available at the correct temperature. · Breastfeeding helps to burn calories. It helps you to lose the weight that you gained during pregnancy. · Breastfeeding makes your uterus return faster to its size before pregnancy. It also slows bleeding (lochia) after
you give birth. · Breastfeeding helps to lower your risk of developing type 2 diabetes, osteoporosis, rheumatoid arthritis, cardiovascular disease, and breast, ovarian, uterine, and endometrial cancer later in life. Breastfeeding basics
Starting breastfeeding · Find a comfortable place to sit or lie down, with your neck and back well-supported. · Place a pillow or a rolled-up blanket under your baby to bring him or her to the level of your breast (if you are
seated). Nursing pillows are specially designed to help support your arms and your baby while you breastfeed. · Make sure that your baby's tummy (abdomen) is facing your abdomen. · Gently massage your breast. With your fingertips, massage from the outer edges of your breast inward toward the
nipple. This encourages milk flow. If your milk flows slowly, you may need to continue this action during the
feeding. · Support your breast with 4 fingers underneath and your thumb above your nipple (make the letter "C" with your
hand). Make sure your fingers are well away from your nipple and your baby’s mouth. · Stroke your baby's lips gently with your finger or nipple. · When your baby's mouth is open wide enough, quickly bring your baby to your breast, placing your entire nipple
and as much of the areola as possible into your baby's mouth. The areola is the colored area around your nipple. Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 390

Baptist Health Louisville
4000 Kresge Way
Louisville KY 40207-4605
Noble, Anella
MRN: [PHONE-842], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F

[DOB-843] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
More Information (continued)
○More areola should be visible above your baby's upper lip than below the lower lip. ○Your baby's lips should be opened and extended outward (flanged) to ensure an adequate, comfortable
latch. ○Your baby's tongue should be between his or her lower gum and your breast. · Make sure that your baby's mouth is correctly positioned around your nipple (latched). Your baby's lips should
create a seal on your breast and be turned out (everted). · It is common for your baby to suck about 2–3 minutes in order to start the flow of breast milk. Latching
Teaching your baby how to latch onto your breast properly is very important. An improper latch can cause nipple pain, decreased milk supply, and poor weight gain in your baby. Also, if your baby is not latched onto your nipple properly, he or she may swallow some air during feeding. This can make your baby fussy. Burping your baby when you switch
breasts during the feeding can help to get rid of the air. However, teaching your baby to latch on properly is still the
best way to prevent fussiness from swallowing air while breastfeeding. Signs that your baby has successfully latched onto your nipple · Silent tugging or silent sucking, without causing you pain. Infant's lips should be extended outward (flanged). · Swallowing heard between every 3–4 sucks once your milk has started to flow (after your let-down milk reflex
occurs). · Muscle movement above and in front of his or her ears while sucking. Signs that your baby has not successfully latched onto your nipple · Sucking sounds or smacking sounds from your baby while breastfeeding. · Nipple pain. If you think your baby has not latched on correctly, slip your finger into the corner of your baby’s mouth to break the
suction and place it between your baby's gums. Attempt to start breastfeeding again. Signs of successful breastfeeding
Signs from your baby · Your baby will gradually decrease the number of sucks or will completely stop sucking. · Your baby will fall asleep. · Your baby's body will relax. · Your baby will retain a small amount of milk in his or her mouth. · Your baby will let go of your breast by himself or herself. Signs from you · Breasts that have increased in firmness, weight, and size 1–3 hours after feeding. · Breasts that are softer immediately after breastfeeding. · Increased milk volume, as well as a change in milk consistency and color by the fifth day of breastfeeding. · Nipples that are not sore, cracked, or bleeding.
Signs that your baby is getting enough milk · Wetting at least 1–2 diapers during the first 24 hours after birth. · Wetting at least 5–6 diapers every 24 hours for the first week after birth. The urine should be clear or pale yellow
by the age of 5 days. · Wetting 6–8 diapers every 24 hours as your baby continues to grow and develop. · At least 3 stools in a 24-hour period by the age of 5 days. The stool should be soft and yellow.
Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 391

Baptist Health Louisville
4000 Kresge Way
Louisville KY 40207-4605

MRN: [PHONE-844], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F

[DOB-845] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
More Information (continued) · At least 3 stools in a 24-hour period by the age of 7 days. The stool should be seedy and yellow. · No loss of weight greater than 10% of birth weight during the first 3 days of life. · Average weight gain of 4–7 oz (113–198 g) per week after the age of 4 days. · Consistent daily weight gain by the age of 5 days, without weight loss after the age of 2 weeks. After a feeding, your baby may spit up a small amount of milk. This is normal. Breastfeeding frequency and duration
Frequent feeding will help you make more milk and can prevent sore nipples and extremely full breasts (breast
engorgement). Breastfeed when you feel the need to reduce the fullness of your breasts or when your baby shows signs
of hunger. This is called "breastfeeding on demand." Signs that your baby is hungry include: · Increased alertness, activity, or restlessness. · Movement of the head from side to side. · Opening of the mouth when the corner of the mouth or cheek is stroked (rooting). · Increased sucking sounds, smacking lips, cooing, sighing, or squeaking. · Hand-to-mouth movements and sucking on fingers or hands. · Fussing or crying. Avoid introducing a pacifier to your baby in the first 4-6 weeks after your baby is born. After this time, you may
choose to use a pacifier. Research has shown that pacifier use during the first year of a baby's life decreases the risk of
sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS).
Allow your baby to feed on each breast as long as he or she wants. When your baby unlatches or falls asleep while
feeding from the first breast, offer the second breast. Because newborns are often sleepy in the first few weeks of life, you may need to awaken your baby to get him or her to feed.
Breastfeeding times will vary from baby to baby. However, the following rules can serve as a guide to help you make
sure that your baby is properly fed: · Newborns (babies 4 weeks of age or younger) may breastfeed every 1–3 hours. · Newborns should not go without breastfeeding for longer than 3 hours during the day or 5 hours during the night. · You should breastfeed your baby a minimum of 8 times in a 24-hour period. Breast milk pumping
Pumping and storing breast milk allows you to make sure that your baby is exclusively fed
your breast milk, even at times when you are unable to breastfeed. This is especially important
if you go back to work while you are still breastfeeding, or if you are not able to be present during
feedings. Your lactation consultant can help you find a method of pumping that works best for you
and give you guidelines about how long it is safe to store breast milk. Caring for your breasts while you breastfeed
Nipples can become dry, cracked, and sore while breastfeeding. The following recommendations can help keep your
breasts moisturized and healthy: · Avoid using soap on your nipples. · Wear a supportive bra designed especially for nursing. Avoid wearing underwire-style bras or extremely tight
Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 392

Baptist Health Louisville
4000 Kresge Way
Louisville KY 40207-4605
Noble, Anella
MRN: [PHONE-846], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F

[DOB-847] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
More Information (continued)
bras (sports bras). · Air-dry your nipples for 3–4 minutes after each feeding. · Use only cotton bra pads to absorb leaked breast milk. Leaking of breast milk between feedings is normal. · Use lanolin on your nipples after breastfeeding. Lanolin helps to maintain your skin's normal moisture barrier. Pure lanolin is not harmful (not toxic) to your baby. You may also hand express a few drops of breast milk and
gently massage that milk into your nipples and allow the milk to air-dry. In the first few weeks after giving birth, some women experience breast engorgement. Engorgement can make your
breasts feel heavy, warm, and tender to the touch. Engorgement peaks within 3–5 days after you give birth. The
following recommendations can help to ease engorgement: · Completely empty your breasts while breastfeeding or pumping. You may want to start by applying warm, moist
heat (in the shower or with warm, water-soaked hand towels) just before feeding or pumping. This increases
circulation and helps the milk flow. If your baby does not completely empty your breasts while breastfeeding, pump any extra milk after he or she is finished. · Apply ice packs to your breasts immediately after breastfeeding or pumping, unless this is too uncomfortable for
you. To do this:
○Put ice in a plastic bag. ○Place a towel between your skin and the bag.
○Leave the ice on for 20 minutes, 2–3 times a day. · Make sure that your baby is latched on and positioned properly while breastfeeding. If engorgement persists after 48 hours of following these recommendations, contact your health care provider or a
lactation consultant. Overall health care recommendations while breastfeeding · Eat 3 healthy meals and 3 snacks every day. Well-nourished mothers who are breastfeeding need an additional
450–500 calories a day. You can meet this requirement by increasing the amount of a balanced diet that you eat. · Drink enough water to keep your urine pale yellow or clear. · Rest often, relax, and continue to take your prenatal vitamins to prevent fatigue, stress, and low vitamin and
mineral levels in your body (nutrient deficiencies). · Do not use any products that contain nicotine or tobacco, such as cigarettes and e-cigarettes. Your baby may be
harmed by chemicals from cigarettes that pass into breast milk and exposure to secondhand smoke. If you need
help quitting, ask your health care provider. · Avoid alcohol. · Do not use illegal drugs or marijuana. · Talk with your health care provider before taking any medicines. These include over-the-counter and
prescription medicines as well as vitamins and herbal supplements. Some medicines that may be harmful to your
baby can pass through breast milk. · It is possible to become pregnant while breastfeeding. If birth control is desired, ask your health care provider
about options that will be safe while breastfeeding your baby. Where to find more information:
La Leche League International: www.llli.org
Contact a health care provider if: · You feel like you want to stop breastfeeding or have become frustrated with breastfeeding. · Your nipples are cracked or bleeding. · Your breasts are red, tender, or warm.

More Information (continued) · You have:
○Painful breasts or nipples. ○A swollen area on either breast.
○A fever or chills. ○Nausea or vomiting. ○Drainage other than breast milk from your nipples. · Your breasts do not become full before feedings by the fifth day after you give birth. · You feel sad and depressed. · Your baby is:
○Too sleepy to eat well.
○Having trouble sleeping. ○More than 1 week old and wetting fewer than 6 diapers in a 24-hour period.
○Not gaining weight by 5 days of age. · Your baby has fewer than 3 stools in a 24-hour period. · Your baby's skin or the white parts of his or her eyes become yellow.
Get help right away if: · Your baby is overly tired (lethargic) and does not want to wake up and feed. · Your baby develops an unexplained fever. Summary · Breastfeeding offers many health benefits for infant and mothers. · Try to breastfeed your infant when he or she shows early signs of hunger. · Gently tickle or stroke your baby's lips with your finger or nipple to allow the baby to open his or her mouth. Bring the baby to your breast. Make sure that much of the areola is in your baby's mouth. Offer one side and burp
the baby before you offer the other side. · Talk with your health care provider or lactation consultant if you have questions or you face problems as you
breastfeed.
This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss
any questions you have with your health care provider. Document Revised: [DOB-850] Document Reviewed: [DOB-851]
